item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Investigations into the visions experienced by the subject of its progeny have been concluded.

Might I suggest "Investigations into the visions of its son experienced by the subject have been concluded" instead? Less confusing to read in that order, and I don't think "progeny" is really appropriate here.

By the way, I instantly got that Body World was the influence for this, and thought that was pretty cool. One of the several Body World exhibits came to my city two years ago and I made a point to go see it. It was a memorable experience to say the least. I can just picture one of the bodies starting to move…(shudder)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this. It seemed to me to be a 'reverse haunting', if you will. The soul is gone, but the body reanimates because there is something left undone in the previous life (fixing his son's errant behavior).
Maybe that's not the way the author intended it, but that's how I took it.

Pretty cool. +1

item.title: Love it
item.content:encoded: I live that there is another scp that can talk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel the same as bogleech. (Weird sentence to type.) It absolutely makes sense to me that a man with such limited biology and no soul would lack the strong emotions we expect of a normal person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Emotions are biochemical; I would totally expect him to be amazingly calm about his state of being.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose it might not be, but that's something I've assumed to be the case ever since I started browsing this site. Oh well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just because 2014 is the current year in real life does not mean that it is the current year in the Foundationverse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, but still. The question remains, why is it forwardly anachronistic - I don't recall any examples of it, unless you count implied examples where future dates are censored like "20██". Authorial intent is indeed irrelevant, but the Foundation's intent is not - "yada yada no canon yada yada" aside, the general trend displayed by other articles is that they try to avoid temporal paradoxes, so anything pertaining to the future is heavily censored; further, the impression of the site as a whole is that the articles are from either the present or the past, so the concept of a document being from the future (without a foreword recognizing it as such), is kind of a head-scratcher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not going to say that that's not what's happening, or that it is, for the liberties of head-canon, but many articles are forwardly-anachronistic.

Related Wikipedia page worth reading: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Authorial_intent

"authorial intent is irrelevant to understanding a work of literature."

Hear that? Literature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Has anyone else noticed that most of the addenda are dated over 10 years in the future? What's going on here, is this supposed to be an "echo" from a SCP-2000 activation that somehow "slipped through the cracks"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral.

It's well written, and its an interesting idea, and there's a lot of little things I like, but there's not enough of a hook for me. I wish a little… more… happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how my reaction to this was, "Aw, poor guy", but not in the usual, "Aw, he's dead and that sucks, poor guy" kind of way. The nonchalance in the interview is actually the selling point for me, and the rest makes for a good narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The most British zombie in the world.

Yes please.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. I don't think this piece went in a very interesting direction. The dialogue seems off (mostly the skip's) and I just can't buy that this guy would be completely unfazed by the fact that he's a skinless zombie. I guess you could say it's because of the absence of his "soul", but even then it still just seems silly to me. The bit about his family didn't get an emotional response out of me at all. Sorry, but it's a downvote from me.

I somehow completely forgot I had already read this and recently read it through it again thinking I hadn't before. For some reason the things about this that were bothering me before aren't bothering me anymore now. I didn't like it enough to upvote but I'm rescinding my downvote appropriately.

Bogleech, I hadn't read your reply before, but looking at it now, that's definitely a valid reason for his attitude. As someone who isn't too well-versed in biology, however, I'm now wondering if he would care at all about his son or the bond he formed with the project head in his state. This isn't anything that sways my feelings on the article one way or the other, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The description of Lexi is enough for you to recognize him, but it's not so detailed and in-depth for the article to constrict a relating audience, which was totally insight, and not oversight that happened to go my way. At all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This gave me some surprisingly deep feels. I could envision the man, and he felt familiar to me. I can't get the idea of this friendly, bearded middle-aged man out of my head. Perhaps he reminded me of my father. But…definitely a little bit of a tearjerker. I had to wipe away a couple little droplets, certainly. This is very well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was a little sad that more wasn't done with the actual anomaly, but the actual content was even more sad. Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I moved to leave it as is, although I understand where people who suggest this are coming from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Following what was considered to be a severe bout of depression, SCP-1697 expired this morning, and is now considered neutralized.

Then shouldn't the class be listed as "Safe Neutralized"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
former head of the Science Museum of Minnesota’s ‘Body World’ exhibit

I dunno if this was a little intentional pun or not, but it made me smile.

Phenomenal. I’m a fan of your professionalism, Doctor.

I now expect a reenactment of "You'll Be a Dentist" from Little Shop of Horrors featuring SCP-1697 and Dr. Holmes.

I tried saying 'Well butter my biscuits and call me Sally, where's my skin', but most of them seemed really put off by that.

I never expected to see that line be used…

And then the rest of it's just really sad in a good way. Well done, Cain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted - just. The comments of Anaxagoras and Eskobar mirror most of what I think is wrong with the article, but the charm of the piece just about won me over. (Plus, science.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of a dead person spontaneously coming back to life is nothing new, but there were enough nice touches throughout to convince me that this has a place on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Repeating the comments I made in chat regarding the use of the phrase "psychic visions" in a Foundation document. Throws the whole tone of the latter half of the document out for me. The Foundation would describe the situation as "the subject came to us and said he experienced sensory hallucinations of etc. etc. etc." rather than "a psychic vision happened". The Foundation does not report what it cannot definitively verify, and Occam's Razor says he hallucinated a hallucination that was remarkably similar to events that happened in real life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Contrary to Weizhong, I'm fine with the nonchalant reaction to reanimation. I find the way it develops with son, psychic visions, etc. to be rather forced and read like desperate clawing for feels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even embalmed zombies get depressed. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spot on, Myriad. Then it went on, unbeknownst to me to replace the 'dr' in "children's", "drugs", and "dry", with Dr.

Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It looks like a bad instance of search and replace. I'm guessing the author tried to replace all instances of dr with Dr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the Dr.. thing a secondary memetic effect? What's the deal with it? It's Dr.iving me crazy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
https://www.google.com/search?q=Body+World&source=lnms&tbm=isch&sa=X&ei=Xw3jUt30BsbhsAS4toKgCg&ved=0CAcQ_AUoAQ&biw=1252&bih=609

Maybe NSFW. They're skinless dead bodies.

That's my inspiration for the bit. Ended up having this draft sit around almost as is for nearly three months. The 'butter my biscuits' part was from Azzleflux, although he was using that as a way to demonstrate to me how inappropriately nonchalant Lexi was in the forums, so there you go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. I like to think I'd be a cool undead person. Pretty satisfied with his demeanor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Didn't really address the issue that I put in chat about Dr. Lexi's extreme nonchalance about being dead, and also missing most of his skin. He just seems so…casual about it.

The little comment he made upon waking up is not what I'd imagine anybody to say when they realized they were a zombie.

-1. I'd be interested in seeing what comes of editing work though.
